metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC7: Add a concrete in-session verifiable done signal — e.g., 'a test asserts `docs/adr/NNNN-opcert-asymmetric.md` exists, is non-empty, and contains the string `ADR 0046`' — matching the pattern AC6 already uses for the migration doc.
findings.finding: AC7 (Docs + ADR) is effort-framed ('write docs covering these topics') with no in-session verifiable observable outcome — unlike AC6, no test assertion or file-existence check is specified, so there is no way to confirm it is done other than subjective review.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7 ('Docs + ADR: environment-attribution model, low/high-security postures…')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC7: 'A test asserts `docs/adr/NNNN-opcert-asymmetric.md` exists, is non-empty, and contains the strings `rebar.opcert.v1`, `trusted_environments.yaml`, and `ADR 0046`.`

findings.finding: The plan calls for routing gate operations to 'a trusted rebar-mcp server (e.g. on AWS)' over the network, but the existing rebar-mcp entry point is exclusively a stdio-transport MCP server (confirmed: build_server().run() with no transport argument, self-described as 'FastMCP, stdio transport'). The plan does not describe how the server is made network-accessible — whether via a new HTTP-transport FastMCP mode, a separate HTTP wrapper, an SSH tunnel, or some other mechanism — leaving the transport adaptation unspecified. This is a new integration surface (networked trusted execution) not previously exercised.
findings.location: Approach §'Trusted remote execution for high assurance'; Scope §'trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution path'; Child task 4

findings.finding: The plan proposes routing gate operations to 'a trusted rebar-mcp server (e.g. on AWS)' over a network, but the existing rebar-mcp server is a stdio-only process (ADR-0007 explicitly states 'exposing it over HTTP is a different transport entirely' and the review-bot was built as a FastAPI app specifically because MCP-over-HTTP would reject webhook requests). No HTTP/streamable transport for rebar-mcp exists in the codebase, and no plan section names what transport would be added or how the developer client connects to a remote rebar-mcp instance.
findings.location: Approach — 'Trusted remote execution for high assurance … a developer routes the gate operation to a trusted rebar-mcp server (e.g. on AWS)'; Scope — 'Trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution path'; AC#5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace every occurrence of `intra_idx` in the plan with `event_position` (the actual field name in `authorship.py`) or explicitly define `intra_idx` as a new field if a different representation is intended, and note the distinction from the authorship path's lexicographic `event_position` comparison.
findings.finding: The plan refers to the merged-log position as `(commit, intra_idx)` in multiple places (Context section, AC4), asserting this is 'the `(commit, intra_idx)` position model the substrate already uses.' Tool inspection finds no field or symbol named `intra_idx` anywhere in the codebase. The real intra-commit ordering in `authorship.py` uses `event_position` — a `{timestamp}-{uuid}` prefix string compared lexicographically. An executor implementing AC4's subject-binding (`Certificates bind {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position (a main commit SHA)}`) will encounter a terminology mismatch and either invent a field or misapply the rule.
findings.location: Context section ('the `(commit, intra_idx)` position model the substrate already uses'); AC4 ('merged-log position (a main commit SHA)'); `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` lines 182–230 (real `event_position` usage)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the verdict string emitted when the required-environment check fails (e.g. `required_env_mismatch`) and add it to both the `verify_signature_result.schema.json` enum and the `src/rebar/types.py` `Literal` — co-locating the decision rule with the schema as AC2 requires.
findings.finding: AC2 mandates a failure verdict when a certificate comes from an environment not in the pinned set ('a test asserts a certificate from an environment not in the pinned set fails the required-environment check'), but the plan never names the verdict string for this failure, and `verify_signature_result.schema.json` has a closed enum of exactly 5 values (`unsigned | foreign_key | certified | mismatch | key_not_valid_at_era`). An executor implementing the required-environment gate must either emit an undocumented string (breaking schema validation for CI consumers) or guess which existing value to reuse — but no decision rule is given.
findings.location: AC2 ('a certificate from an environment not in the pinned set fails the required-environment check'); `src/rebar/schemas/verify_signature_result.schema.json` line 17 (closed enum); `src/rebar/types.py` line 237 (Literal type mirrors the closed enum)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"required_env_mismatch"` to the `verify_signature_result.schema.json` enum array and the `Literal` in `types.py`, and name that string explicitly in AC2 so child 2 and the CI consumer (child 3) both reference the same vocabulary.

findings.finding: The required-environment check introduces at least two new failure outcomes — 'environment not in pinned set' and 'required certificate missing' — but no verdict string is defined for them anywhere in the plan, and the existing verify_signature_result.schema.json enum (unsigned | foreign_key | certified | mismatch | key_not_valid_at_era) has no slots for them.
findings.location: AC2 ('a test asserts a certificate from an environment not in the pinned set fails the required-environment check') and AC3 ('fails when a ticket that must carry one does not'); verify_signature_result.schema.json verdict enum
findings.suggested_fix: Add at minimum `env_cert_missing` and `env_not_trusted` to the verdict enum in `verify_signature_result.schema.json` (mirroring how `key_not_valid_at_era` was added by the identity epic), and reference those strings by name in AC2 and AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the KMS/Ed25519 incompatibility in AC5 and child ee0b: either (a) specify AWS Secrets Manager (not KMS) as the key store, acknowledge the key is exportable to authorized admins, and update the assurance claim accordingly; or (b) change the signing scheme to one KMS does support (e.g. ECC P-256 with a custom DSSE scheme) and update the substrate + POLICY table to match.
findings.finding: AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 asymmetric signing, making the plan's AC5 / child ee0b statement that the trusted MCP server's signing key lives in 'a secrets manager / KMS' mechanistically incorrect for the SSHSIG scheme.
findings.location: AC5 ('The AWS MCP-server infra declares … stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS'); child ee0b AC; Approach section ('trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution … KMS/secrets')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the AWS KMS / Ed25519 capability mismatch in AC5 / child 4: either (a) adopt ECDSA P-256 (KMS-native, but requires updating AC1/AC4/trust-config schema and the SSHSIG allowed_signers format) or (b) keep Ed25519 and store the private key in AWS Secrets Manager with envelope encryption, explicitly acknowledging the loss of HSM-backed non-exportability and adding compensating controls (IAM, rotation runbook).
findings.finding: AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 as a managed signing key type. The plan's AC5 / child 4 asserts the trusted rebar-mcp server's signing key is stored in 'a secrets manager / KMS', but throughout the plan the signing key is Ed25519. AWS KMS's asymmetric SIGN_VERIFY key specs are limited to ECDSA (ECC_NIST_P256/P384/P521), RSA (2048–4096), and SM2 — Ed25519 is not in the supported key spec list. This means KMS cannot act as the HSM-backed signing primitive for an Ed25519 environment key, which is the plan's stated key type.
findings.location: AC5 / child 4 (Trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution path + AWS infra): 'stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC5 and child 4's scope, replace 'KMS' with 'AWS Secrets Manager (with KMS-managed envelope encryption)' for the Ed25519 private key, add a note that KMS asymmetric signing does not support Ed25519, and document the compensating control (IAM least-privilege + key rotation runbook per L4's deferred-hardening note in ADR 0046). Alternatively, if HSM-backed signing is a hard requirement, adopt ECC_NIST_P256 as the environment key type and update AC1/AC4/trust-config schema accordingly — noting that SSHSIG/allowed_signers natively supports ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the Ed25519/KMS incompatibility in AC5 and child 4: either (a) accept that the signing key lives in AWS Secrets Manager (not KMS) with instance-side signing and name compensating controls (key access policy, instance role scope, rotation procedure), or (b) switch the MCP server signing scheme to ECDSA P-256 (KMS-supported) and specify how the MCP server calls KMS Sign directly — noting this diverges from the sshsig/Ed25519 scheme used elsewhere and must be reconciled in the registry.
findings.finding: AC5 and child 4 (ee0b) specify the MCP server signing key be stored in 'secrets manager / KMS', but AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 for sign/verify operations (KMS supports RSA, ECDSA P-256/P-384/secp256k1, and SM2 only). Using KMS natively for Ed25519 signing is impossible; using Secrets Manager to store raw Ed25519 private key material means the key is loaded into process memory on the instance, not HSM-protected — a materially different security posture than 'KMS'. The plan does not resolve this contradiction.
findings.location: AC5 / child 4 (ee0b) / Approach 'stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the signing-key protection mechanism in AC5/child-4: if the intent is SSM SecureString storage with local signing (matching the existing `mcp-hmac-signing-key` infra pattern), say so explicitly and drop the 'KMS' signing claim; if hardware-protected signing is required, name a feasible path (e.g. AWS CloudHSM + PKCS#11 + ssh-keygen's PKCS#11 provider, or accept that Ed25519-in-KMS is not available and use a KMS-supported curve instead).
findings.finding: AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 asymmetric signing. The plan's AC5/child-4 states the trusted MCP-server 'stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS' — but if 'KMS' means AWS KMS performing the signing operation, that is a capability gap: AWS KMS asymmetric signing key types are RSA, ECC NIST (P-256/384/521), and SM2 — Ed25519 (Curve25519) is absent. The existing infra pattern (ssm.tf) uses SSM SecureString + kms:Decrypt on the AWS-managed SSM key for *storage* of plain secrets (not KMS-native signing). If the intent is SSM storage with local signing (as in the existing `mcp-hmac-signing-key` pattern), that is feasible but the 'KMS' framing is contradicted for native Ed25519 signing and should be corrected to avoid child-4 implementers targeting a non-existent KMS signing path.
findings.location: AC5 / child 4 / Approach section: 'stores the signing key in a secrets manager / KMS'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'secrets manager / KMS' in AC5 with the concrete pattern: 'stored as an SSM SecureString (encrypted at rest by the AWS-managed SSM key; IAM-scoped to the MCP instance role) and loaded into memory at server startup — the private key is never written to disk unencrypted.' If KMS-native signing is a hard requirement, note that AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 and the substrate's SSHSIG scheme would need a replacement or the key curve changed to P-256 (which KMS does support).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the KMS/Ed25519 incompatibility in AC5 and child 4: either (a) specify that the signing key uses AWS Secrets Manager (not KMS HSM-backed signing) and document the resulting custody tradeoff, or (b) change the signing algorithm to ECDSA P-256/P-384 (KMS-signable) and update AC1/the SSHSIG substrate accordingly.
findings.finding: AC5 specifies that the trusted MCP server stores and uses its signing key in 'a secrets manager / KMS', but AWS KMS does not support Ed25519 asymmetric signing operations. KMS supports RSA, ECDSA (P-256/P-384), and SM2 — not Ed25519. This means if the signing key must be Ed25519 (as AC1 specifies), KMS cannot perform the signing operation; the key material would need to be stored in AWS Secrets Manager and loaded into process memory for signing — a materially weaker custody model than HSM-backed KMS signing.
findings.location: AC5 / AC1 / Intended decomposition child 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC5: Name the specific authentication mechanism for the trusted MCP-server endpoint (e.g., AWS IAM SigV4 request signing, mTLS with a client cert pinned in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, or similar) so the executor need not choose.
findings.finding: AC5's AWS endpoint authentication mechanism is unspecified ('declares human/admin authentication') — the executor must choose among mTLS, OAuth, API key, IAM SigV4, etc. without guidance, which is a real design decision not a convention.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 ('The AWS MCP-server infra declares human/admin authentication for its endpoint…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'endpoint authentication uses [mechanism, e.g. AWS IAM SigV4 with a dedicated IAM role per caller]; IaC declares an IAM policy granting only the signing-key KMS action and the repo-read permission to that role.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the human/admin authentication mechanism for the trusted rebar-mcp server endpoint in AC5 and the Approach section (e.g., 'IAM SigV4 + VPC-private endpoint', 'OIDC/OAuth2 via AWS Cognito or GitHub IdP', 'mTLS with client-cert per-developer', or 'private-network-only — no public endpoint, accessed via SSM Session Manager or VPN only'); the mechanism must cover both developer callers and admin operators.
findings.finding: The trusted rebar-mcp server is a new internet-reachable AWS service (child 4 / AC5), but the plan never names the human/admin authentication mechanism for its endpoint. AC5 says it 'declares human/admin authentication for its endpoint' — this is a commitment to specify, not the specification. No protocol (OIDC/OAuth, mTLS, IAM SigV4, API key, VPN-only, etc.) appears anywhere in the plan, Approach section, Scope, or child ticket ee0b.
findings.location: AC5 / child 4 (ee0b) / 'Trusted remote execution for high assurance' in Approach
findings.suggested_fix: If the MCP server is only reachable by developers' AI agents (not general internet), the cleanest option consistent with the existing IAM model is IAM SigV4 authentication via an API Gateway + Lambda or ALB + AWS_IAM auth, with per-developer IAM identities scoped to the `rebar:opcert:request` action. Admin access stays via SSM Session Manager (matching the Gerrit pattern). Add a single sentence to AC5: 'Developer callers authenticate via [mechanism]; admin shell access is via AWS SSM Session Manager (no inbound SSH).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the specific authentication mechanism (e.g., IAM SigV4, mTLS with client certs, OIDC, or named API-key scheme) for the trusted rebar-mcp signing endpoint in AC5 and child 4's scope, not just the category ('human/admin authentication').
findings.finding: The trusted rebar-mcp signing server (child 4 / AC5) is a new network-reachable AWS service, but its endpoint's authentication mechanism is never declared — AC5 says 'declares human/admin authentication for its endpoint' without naming any protocol (mTLS, OAuth, IAM SigV4, API key, OIDC, etc.). This is an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: AC5 / Intended decomposition child 4 / Approach 'Trusted remote execution for high assurance'
findings.suggested_fix: Given the existing AWS/IAM infrastructure, IAM SigV4 request signing (with the caller assuming a scoped role via STS) is a natural fit: add to AC5 'endpoint requires AWS SigV4-signed requests from callers holding a scoped IAM role (arn:aws:iam::<acct>:role/rebar-opcert-requester); unauthenticated requests are rejected 403.' This keeps the auth mechanism in the existing IAM trust model and eliminates the need for a separate secret.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan that the contract (HMAC removal) step MUST NOT deploy until a verified census confirms zero active tickets carry HMAC-signed op-certs, document the rollback state explicitly ('after rollback of the contract step, the HMAC verification path is restored; pre-existing HMAC certs remain verifiable'), and include a keep-alive / rollback restoration test for the hmac_legacy scheme.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback statement for HMAC removal ('disabling it reverts to local behavior') refers only to the require_environment policy toggle, NOT to the HMAC scheme removal. Once hmac_legacy.py is deleted (contract phase), pre-existing HMAC-signed SIGNATURE events on the tickets branch become permanently unverifiable — a code rollback does not restore those certs. The plan never asserts the schema state after a rollback of the contract step, nor guarantees all HMAC certs are re-issued before deletion. DATA LOSS RISK.
findings.location: AC6 / 'HMAC removal (contract)' / Rollback note in AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule to the Trust config & era-validity protocol section specifying what happens when `added_at_commit` or `revoked_at_commit` cannot be resolved by `git merge-base` (e.g., 'treat as key-not-valid / fail-closed, surface a `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict with a reason naming the unresolvable SHA').
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the era-validity rule for op-certs reuses the ancestry predicate but provides no fallback behavior for the case where an explicit `added_at_commit` or `revoked_at_commit` SHA is unresolvable by `git merge-base --is-ancestor` (e.g., the SHA is not reachable in the local clone, git fails, or the config records a typo). The authorship path's current fallback is to return `None` from `resolve_event_commit` and silently skip the key (treating it as not yet added), but for op-certs the explicit-SHA path has no equivalent resolver and the plan never states whether an unresolvable SHA means 'key invalid', 'key valid', or 'error/fail-closed'.
findings.location: Trust config & era-validity protocol section ('A config key is valid at P iff git merge-base --is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P) is true AND …'); AC4 ('Environment-key rotation uses explicit-SHA era-scoped validity'); `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` lines 212–215 (current None-skips-key fallback)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Trust config & era-validity protocol section, add: 'If `git merge-base --is-ancestor` exits non-zero for any reason other than "not an ancestor" (e.g., unknown object, git error), the key MUST be treated as not valid at P (fail-closed); the verifier surfaces `key_not_valid_at_era` with a reason noting the unresolvable SHA.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the migration artifact (and the plan's HMAC removal section) an explicit dual-write window protocol: define the cutover criterion (e.g. 'all environments have deployed the new signing code AND all HMAC certs have been re-issued') before the HMAC verification path is removed, and specify how multi-environment deployments at different versions coexist safely during the window.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the dual-write window mechanics during the HMAC→Ed25519 coexistence period: what happens to SIGNATURE events written by environments at different upgrade stages (one writing Ed25519, another still reading only HMAC), and what the safe-cutover criterion is before the HMAC scheme is removed.
findings.location: AC6 / child 6 (HMAC removal) / 'HMAC removal (contract) + expand-contract migration artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a three-phase gate: (1) ship Ed25519 signing + keep HMAC verifier live ('expand'); (2) re-issue all HMAC certs as Ed25519 — only proceed to step 3 when zero HMAC-signed certs remain on any active ticket; (3) remove HMAC verification ('contract'). The CI job should accept both algorithms during phase 2 and gate phase 3 on a census showing no surviving HMAC certs.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify how SIGNATURE events written DURING the cutover window are handled. During the migration window, the system may accept either HMAC or Ed25519 certs (the dual-accept phase), but the plan gives no policy for which scheme new writes use, whether the gate verifiers accept both during the window, or how the transition from dual-accept to Ed25519-only is triggered.
findings.location: AC6 ('documented cutover window') / Scope ('HMAC removal… expand-contract migration')

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the HMAC-removal contract phase to child 6 and the migration artifact spec: specify whether the HMAC verifier can be re-enabled via a flag/config (temporary re-enablement during rollback), what happens to asymmetric certs already issued during the transition if the binary rolls back, and the operator recovery procedure if re-issue is incomplete at cutover.
findings.finding: The HMAC removal ('contract' phase) is a destructive and practically irreversible operation — once deployed, existing HMAC-signed op-certs cannot be re-verified and a version rollback would leave asymmetric certs unverifiable by the old verifier. The plan names expand-contract and requires a migration doc covering the 'cutover window / re-issue procedure,' but never specifies the *rollback mechanism for the contract (removal) phase itself*: what operators do if they must roll back the binary after HMAC is removed, how already-issued asymmetric certs are handled, and whether the old HMAC verifier can be re-enabled without a code change. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not just acknowledgement of the expand-contract approach.
findings.location: AC — HMAC removal item; child 6 (HMAC removal + expand-contract migration artifact)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'rollback window' to the expand-contract spec: the HMAC verifier is retained (but signing disabled) for N releases after the cutover version, allowing emergency rollback to re-verify old certs; the migration doc must name this window. Remove the verifier only after the window expires. Update the AC to require the migration doc to name both the cutover version AND the removal version (i.e., the end of the rollback window).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the four-phase deploy order (new kind → rebar-mcp server → CI gate enable → HMAC contract/removal), declare the coexistence window (e.g., 'the new code MUST continue to verify HMAC-signed certs until the named cutover version'), and add a one-step rollback path for the CI gate (e.g., 'set `ENFORCE_SINCE` to a future date to disable enforcement') and rebar-mcp server (IaC revert procedure).
findings.finding: The plan names an expand-contract HMAC migration and an rebar-mcp AWS server but does not specify: (a) the deploy ordering across the four phases (ship new `rebar.opcert.v1` kind → deploy rebar-mcp server → enable CI gate enforcement → execute HMAC removal/contract step), (b) the coexistence behavior during the expand phase (whether the new code continues to accept and verify existing HMAC-signed op-certs while projects re-issue certs, or immediately rejects them), and (c) the rollback path for the CI gate enforcement step (CI config revert) and the rebar-mcp server (IaC revert). Without this, the contract step risks a flag-day break for any project that upgrades before re-issuing all certs.
findings.location: Scope / AC item 6 (HMAC removal) / Intended decomposition children 4 & 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Migration & deploy ordering' subsection to Scope or the HMAC removal AC: (1) Phase 1 (expand): ship `rebar.opcert.v1`; `hmac_legacy.py` remains in `POLICY` table as a read-only verifier — new certs are signed asymmetrically, old HMAC certs continue to verify. (2) Phase 2: deploy rebar-mcp server; run smoke tests. (3) Phase 3: enable CI gate with `ENFORCE_SINCE` set to a future date, giving projects a re-issue window. (4) Phase 4 (contract): after the cutover version/date, remove `hmac_legacy.py` from `POLICY`. Rollback for phases 2–4: revert CI `ENFORCE_SINCE` / IaC tag; rollback for phase 4: revert the `POLICY` table deletion.

findings.finding: AC4 specifies that certificates bind `{ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position}` but never defines the payload schema: field names, types, canonical serialization format, or what 'material fingerprint' means in the op-cert context (the existing codebase uses it specifically for plan-review description+ACs, not generically).
findings.location: AC4 ('Certificates bind {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position}'); Approach section ('Certificates bind {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position} so they cannot be replayed')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retention rule to the trust-config schema: specify whether key entries with `revoked_at_commit` set must be retained permanently (to support historical two-phase `key_not_valid_at_era` classification), or explicitly document that revoked-key pruning degrades historical cert diagnostics to `bad_signature` (acceptable or not).
findings.finding: The two-phase `key_not_valid_at_era` vs `bad_signature` verdict for op-certs requires verifying a certificate against all historical keys for the env_id (including revoked ones), but the plan does not specify whether revoked keys must be retained in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` permanently, creating a silent breakage when operators prune them.
findings.location: AC4 ('a test asserts a certificate from a key valid at its era verifies while one past its rotation surfaces key_not_valid_at_era'); child 368c description ('Two-phase verdict … first verify against ANY historical key for the env; if that passes but the era-scoped check fails, emit key_not_valid_at_era'); Trust config schema section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Trust config schema section: 'Entries with a non-null `revoked_at_commit` MUST be retained indefinitely; removing them degrades the historical two-phase verdict to `bad_signature` rather than `key_not_valid_at_era`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete integration mechanism for the op-cert merge-gate lane: either (a) a new flag to the `rebar verify-identity` command that also checks op-cert required-environment attestations in the same traversal, or (b) a second `rebar verify-opcerts` step added to the same `verify-identity.yaml` job (sharing the job's checkout and ENFORCE_SINCE but as a distinct command), with a clear statement of which is preferred and why.
findings.finding: The plan says child 3 (merge-gate CI) 'extends the shipped `verify-identity.yaml` gate' with 'one merged-log walk', but the shipped gate runs `rebar verify-identity` which walks tickets-branch event files for `author_sig`, while op-cert verification queries the `attestations` map — a structurally different data traversal. The plan leaves the integration mechanism entirely unspecified, creating an implementation ambiguity that child 4214 does not resolve.
findings.location: AC3 ('extends the shipped `verify-identity.yaml` merge-gate (same merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering) rather than a parallel job'); child 4214 description and AC; Scope section ('extend the shipped `verify-identity` gate; do not stand up a parallel merged-log walker')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan (Scope or child 4 AC) whether the MCP server IaC is added to the existing `infra/terraform/` root module or a new root; if a new root, require it to declare an S3 remote backend with `use_lockfile = true` and confirm the terraform-drift CI job's path filter covers it.
findings.finding: The plan calls for 'IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment' (child 4 / Scope section) but does not state whether the new MCP server IaC will be added to the existing `infra/terraform/` root module (which has S3 remote state, S3-native locking, and the terraform-drift CI job) or stand up a new root. If a new root, the remote-state backend, locking discipline, and drift-detection CI coverage must be explicitly carried over; without this, the new infra could silently use local state.
findings.location: Scope section ('IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment') / child 4 (ee0b)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to child 4 scope: 'MCP server IaC lives in `infra/terraform/` alongside the Gerrit module (same S3 remote state + drift CI coverage) unless a new root is justified, in which case a backend block with S3 + use_lockfile=true is required and the terraform-drift.yml path filter must be updated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit ordering constraint: child 8f1d depends on child 42d1 (the required-environment policy must exist before 8f1d's rollback AC can be verified), or move the rollback AC into 42d1 where the policy is owned.
findings.finding: Child 8f1d AC3 ('Rollback: the required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off; disabling it reverts to local behavior. A test asserts the default-off path still produces/reads op-certs locally') tests behavior that is delivered by child 42d1 (which owns the policy and its default-off semantics). No ordering constraint between 8f1d and 42d1 is stated; if 8f1d is implemented or evaluated before 42d1, its rollback AC cannot be verified.
findings.location: child 8f1d-72d5-d8b1-400f
findings.suggested_fix: Move 8f1d AC3's rollback assertion into child 42d1 (as: 'A test asserts absent .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml / no require_environment defaults to local op-cert behavior'), and add a note in 8f1d that rollback is contingent on 42d1's implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'keyid is an unauthenticated hint' and 'env_id MUST equal the DSSE principal (keyid)' by specifying the exact lookup sequence: state whether the verifier iterates all config env_id entries to find a matching public key, uses keyid only as an advisory hint for performance, and confirms that the trust anchor is always the pinned public key — not the cert's self-claimed keyid.
findings.finding: The Prior art section calls DSSE keyid 'an unauthenticated hint — verify against pinned keys,' and the Approach section says 'verify against the pinned key, not the claimed key_id.' Yet the Trust config schema specifies that env_id 'MUST equal the DSSE principal (keyid).' These two statements contradict each other: if keyid is an unauthenticated hint that must not be trusted for lookup, the MUST-equal constraint implies the implementer CAN use keyid as a lookup key (defeating the pinned-key model). An implementer reading both sections cannot determine whether verification starts from the config's env_id (trusted) or from the cert's claimed keyid (untrusted).
findings.location: Approach section ('Verify against the pinned key, not the claimed key_id') vs. Trust config & era-validity protocol section, Schema block ('env_id … MUST equal the DSSE principal (keyid)') vs. Prior art section ('DSSE (keyid is an unauthenticated hint — verify against pinned keys)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Trust config section: 'The verifier iterates entries in trusted_environments.yaml by env_id (not by the cert's self-claimed keyid); keyid in the DSSE envelope MAY be used as a hint to short-circuit the scan but is not trusted. The env_id MUST equal the DSSE principal solely to ensure the cert's claimed principal can be cross-checked after the pinned-key verification succeeds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements to AC5 or child 4: at minimum a CloudWatch alarm on the MCP server health (availability/error rate) wired to the existing SNS topic, CloudWatch log group for the server's signing operations, and a metric for signing errors — consistent with the Gerrit monitoring baseline in `infra/terraform/monitoring*.tf`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies IaC for the trusted rebar-mcp server but names no observability requirements (logging, metrics, alarms) for it, unlike the Gerrit server which has detailed CloudWatch alarms (gate-down, EC2 status checks, disk pressure, replication errors). A signing service that silently stops responding or producing certificates would leave high-assurance projects with a broken gate and no alerting.
findings.location: AC5 / child 4 (ee0b) / Scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the migration artifact spec (AC6) that the re-issue procedure must be idempotent (skip tickets already carrying a valid Ed25519 cert) and resumable from a checkpoint, so a partial run can be safely retried without double-signing.
findings.finding: The plan mentions a 're-issue procedure' in the migration artifact for existing HMAC-signed certs but does not specify whether the re-issuance process is idempotent and resumable if interrupted across a large number of tickets.
findings.location: AC6: 'A named migration artifact docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md exists documenting the expand-contract cutover window / re-issue procedure'
findings.suggested_fix: The re-issuer should check whether a ticket's latest SIGNATURE event already has algorithm=Ed25519/DSSE before writing a new one; if so, skip it. A simple cursor (last processed ticket id or alphabetic position) enables resumability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that child 1 (368c) must first extract `_is_ancestor` (currently a private closure in `verify_authorship_at_commit`) to a stable module-level function in `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` (or a new `attest/_ancestry.py`) before adding op-cert era-validity logic, and name the target module and function signature in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan describes the `git merge-base --is-ancestor` check as already 'factored so both authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) call one shared ancestry predicate,' but tool inspection shows `_is_ancestor` is a module-private inner closure defined only inside `verify_authorship_at_commit` in `authorship.py` (line 196). It has no stable callable surface — it is not exported, not a module-level function, and not reusable by child 1 (368c) without either duplicating it or restructuring `authorship.py`. The plan's factored-predicate protocol is referenced but not yet co-located with any public API.
findings.location: Approach section ('factored so both authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) call one shared ancestry predicate'); `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` lines 196–203 (`_is_ancestor` private inner closure); AC4 ('reusing the commit-ancestry era rule')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a design note to child 1 (368c): expose `is_ancestor(ancestor: str, descendant: str, *, repo_root=None) -> bool` at module level in `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` (or `attest/_ancestry.py`) as the first step, then have both `verify_authorship_at_commit` and the new op-cert verifier call it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] (Advisory) Consider whether child 4 (imminent-reformable-fruitbat) should separate the rebar-mcp authoritative-fetch application logic from the AWS IaC/deployment, given they have different actors (dev vs. ops), different change frequencies, and the logic is independently testable — or document why bundling is the right call.
findings.finding: Child 4 (imminent-reformable-fruitbat) bundles two concerns with different actors and change kinds: (a) the rebar-mcp authoritative-state-fetch application logic and (b) the AWS IaC/cloud deployment (IAM, KMS, endpoint auth). These have different reasons to change — the fetch protocol evolves with the op-cert design; the infra evolves with cloud/org policy — and the application logic is independently testable (against a local or mocked server) without the IaC. This is an advisory diffusion signal worth the author considering, though the two are order-dependent enough that bundling may be the right call.
findings.location: Intended decomposition (children) — child 4 (imminent-reformable-fruitbat); AC 'Trusted rebar-mcp remote path…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to child 6a89 requiring the ADR to cross-reference ADR 0046 (security posture; L2/L4 are op-cert-scoped), and a test/assertion that the reference exists.
findings.finding: Child 6a89's acceptance criteria omit the ADR 0046 cross-reference that the parent AC7 explicitly requires ('Cross-references ADR 0046 (security posture; L2/L4 are op-cert-scoped)'). No other sibling in the roster covers this. This is a scope-narrowing gap: child 6a89 covers all listed doc topics but does not bind the ADR to the mandatory cross-reference.
findings.location: child 6a89-1165-ea58-4be3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 6a89's AC: '[ ] The ADR cross-references ADR 0046 (security posture), noting that L2/L4 are op-cert-scoped; a test asserts the cross-reference string is present in the ADR file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign to one child (368c or 8f1d) an explicit AC to update plan-review and completion-verifier tools to invoke opcert.py (the new rebar.opcert.v1 kind) instead of the signing.py HMAC path.
findings.finding: No child explicitly owns wiring the plan-review and completion-verifier tools from the legacy signing.py HMAC path to the new opcert.py substrate. Child 8f1d removes the HMAC sign/verify path in signing.py, and child 368c creates opcert.py, but neither child names the caller migration — i.e., which child updates the plan-review and completion-verifier tools to call opcert.py instead of signing.py. This is an owned-by-none consumer-impact gap.
findings.location: child 8f1d-72d5-d8b1-400f
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 368c or 8f1d: '[ ] The plan-review and completion-verifier tool entry-points are updated to call opcert.py (rebar.opcert.v1) for signing and verification; a test asserts the tools produce an asymmetric op-cert end-to-end.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to child ee0b (or a new child) for the client-side routing: the developer tooling must be updated to offer/invoke the trusted rebar-mcp remote path, and a test asserts the local vs. remote path selection.
findings.finding: Child ee0b covers the trusted rebar-mcp server-side path (fetching authoritative state and signing), but no child explicitly owns the client-side routing — the developer-facing tooling change that routes a gate operation to the trusted remote server instead of running locally. The parent scope says 'a developer routes the gate operation to a trusted rebar-mcp server,' implying client-side invocation logic, but no child AC claims this.
findings.location: child ee0b-c5e2-3454-42f1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child ee0b AC: '[ ] The rebar client tooling exposes a flag or config option to route a gate operation to the trusted rebar-mcp endpoint; a test asserts the remote path is invoked when configured and the local path is used otherwise.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts era-scoped key validity is achieved by 'reusing the commit-ancestry era mechanism and the `_keyring_for()` / `verify_authorship_at_commit()` machinery in `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py`' — but that machinery reads keyring records from a rebar *identity ticket* (`ticket.get('keyring')`). Environment public keys are explicitly out-of-band (a 'Gerrit-protected / CI config path, NOT the tickets branch'). There is no shipped or described environment-keyring storage format, location, or indexing structure that `_keyring_for()` or an analogous function could read. The plan claims reuse but leaves the environment-keyring storage model entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section ('Environment-key rotation reuses the era-scoped key-validity mechanism … `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit`'); AC4 ('reusing the commit-ancestry era mechanism … a test asserts a certificate from a key valid at its era verifies while one past its rotation does not'); src/rebar/attest/authorship.py:115-128 (_keyring_for loads from ticket 'keyring' field)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC3: Specify the artifact form for the CI-trigger audit (e.g., a comment block in the workflow YAML, a table in the ADR, or a test that enumerates `.github/workflows/*.yaml` triggers and asserts each is either included in the op-cert gate or has a documented exclusion reason) so 'classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER' is in-session verifiable.
findings.finding: AC3's CI-trigger audit phrase 'every existing workflow trigger that should fire on the gate is classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER' does not specify what artifact 'classified' produces — there is no named file, comment convention, or test assertion that would let an executor know when the audit is done.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('every existing workflow trigger that should fire on the gate is classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER')
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'documented in a table in `docs/adr/NNNN-opcert-gate.md` with columns [workflow file | trigger | INCLUDED/EXCLUDED | reason]; a test asserts the table file exists and no workflow trigger is undocumented.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'INCLUDED' and 'NO_FILTER' in the plan (or replace them with plain-language equivalents), and specify what artifact records the classification (e.g. a comment block in the workflow YAML, a table in the ADR, or a test fixture) so AC3's trigger-audit sub-requirement is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC3 requires that 'every existing workflow trigger that should fire on the gate is classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER,' but the terms INCLUDED and NO_FILTER are never defined anywhere in the plan. No section explains what these classifications mean, what entity produces them, or what action the implementer must take to satisfy them. A reviewer or implementer cannot determine what the acceptance criterion requires.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 (merge-gate CI job bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER' with 'documented as either triggering the gate (INCLUDED) or explicitly excluded with a rationale (EXCLUDED), recorded in a table in the ADR or as inline comments in the workflow YAML.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in child 7 / the docs AC whether ADR 0046 is to be superseded, amended, or annotated with a forward-reference to the new ADR — do not leave ADR 0046 as an unmodified stale document after this epic ships.
findings.finding: ADR 0046 is described as covering 'security posture; L2/L4 are op-cert-scoped' and this epic fundamentally changes the op-cert security model (HMAC→asymmetric, local→environment-attributed, optional remote gate). The plan says the new ADR 'cross-references ADR 0046' but does not indicate whether ADR 0046 itself is updated, superseded, or annotated — leaving it as a stale reference that describes the old HMAC-based model as current.
findings.location: AC — Docs + ADR item; Scope section; Approach section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Docs + ADR AC: 'ADR 0046 is updated to mark sections L2/L4 as superseded by [new ADR number] and to add a forward-reference; alternatively a new ADR supersedes 0046 in full with an explicit Supersedes header.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and mitigate the coupling of authorship + op-cert verification in the extended `verify-identity.yaml` job — e.g., specify whether they run as separate steps, separate jobs sharing an action/library, or a single combined step, and describe how a failure in one kind is isolated from the other.
findings.finding: The plan prescribes extending the shipped `verify-identity.yaml` CI job to also enforce op-cert verification, but does not acknowledge or mitigate the coupling risk: a bug or regression in the new op-cert verification step could fail the existing authorship `Verified` gate for unrelated PRs, and changes to either verification kind could unintentionally affect the other.
findings.location: Scope section / AC bullet 3 / Intended decomposition child 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add the op-cert check as a separate, named job (e.g., `verify-opcert`) in the same workflow file that shares the merged-log walk via a reusable composite action or script, rather than inlining it into the existing `verify-identity` job steps. This preserves independent failure signals while reusing the walk infrastructure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which module owns the shared commit-ancestry era-validity predicate (e.g., a new `src/rebar/attest/ancestry.py`), so that neither `authorship.py` nor the op-cert module depends on the other, preserving the orthogonal actor separation at the code level.
findings.finding: The plan says the commit-ancestry era-validity predicate in `authorship.py` will be 'factored so both authorship and op-cert call one shared ancestry predicate,' but does not specify which module will own the shared predicate — leaving an implicit coupling where `authorship.py` (an identity-epic module) may become a dependency of the op-cert module, inverting or blurring the intended separation between the two signing actors.
findings.location: Trust config & era-validity protocol section / Intended decomposition child 1
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the `git merge-base --is-ancestor` predicate and `key_not_valid_at_era` verdict logic into `src/rebar/attest/ancestry.py` (or similar neutral utility module under `attest/`), imported by both `authorship.py` and the new op-cert module. Name this extraction explicitly in child 1's scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve child 5893's status: either formally close it as superseded by 368c (with a note that 368c's ACs subsume it) or remove it from the active sibling roster and update the parent decomposition accordingly.
findings.finding: Child 5893 is presented as a live child with its own acceptance criteria (era-scoped rotation test + documentation) despite the parent epic explicitly marking it '~~Env-key era-scoped rotation~~ — folded into child 1 (former task 5893, dropped as superseded).' Child 368c fully subsumes 5893's ACs. The simultaneous existence of both creates a direct scope overlap: two children claim to deliver the era-rotation test ('a cert from a key valid at its era verifies while one past its rotation does not'), and child 5893's AC2 ('rotation is documented') partially overlaps with child 6a89 (docs+ADR).
findings.suggested_fix: Close child 5893 as 'superseded by 368c' with an explicit note that 368c AC3 delivers the full era-rotation test. Remove 5893 from the active sibling roster. If the 'no trusted-clock dependency' assertion from 5893 AC2 is considered important, add it as a sub-bullet to 368c AC3.

findings.finding: The plan says it 'reuses' authorship.py's commit-ancestry era-validity mechanism for environment-key rotation, but that mechanism (resolve_event_commit) resolves key-add/revoke positions by globbing `<ticket_dir>/<position>-*.json` files on the tracker branch. Environment keys live in an out-of-band config path (explicitly 'NOT the tickets branch'), so there are no ticket-dir event files to glob. The plan does not specify how the era mechanism is adapted to the out-of-band storage model.
findings.location: Approach section ('Environment-key rotation reuses the era-scoped key-validity mechanism the identity epic shipped in src/rebar/attest/authorship.py'); AC4 ('Environment-key rotation uses era-scoped validity... reusing the commit-ancestry era mechanism')

findings.finding: The out-of-band trusted-environment key config — referenced as the trust root for the entire policy — has no defined schema: no file path, format (YAML/JSON/TOML), or field vocabulary (`env_id`, `public_key`, `era` bounds) is specified anywhere in the plan. This is the schema that child tickets 2, 3, and 4 must all read consistently.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #2 / Approach 'Per-project optional requirement' / Scope 'out-of-band environment public-key trust config'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Trust config format' subsection to the Approach or Scope section: 'Stored at `<repo-root>/.rebar/trusted_envs.yaml`; YAML list of `{env_id: str, public_key: str [base64], era: {from_log_pos: int, to_log_pos: int|null}}`; path is CODEOWNERS-protected and must never be on the auto-pushed tickets branch.'

findings.finding: The era-scoped `valid-at-log-position` validity decision rule is referenced as a novel key-rotation semantic but the algorithm — how a verifier determines the log position to use, how era boundaries are stored and looked up, and what happens when two eras overlap — is never defined and is not co-located with any schema that uses it. This is a protocol an executor must implement, not a metaphor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #4 / Approach 'Verify against the pinned key' / Intended decomposition child 5

findings.finding: The out-of-band trusted-environment public key config is described by where it must NOT live (not the tickets branch) and by governance properties (review-gated), but neither its file path, file format (JSON/YAML/OpenSSH authorized_keys), nor its schema is specified; an executor cannot write AC #2's pinned-key tests without resolving these.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2; Approach — 'Per-project optional requirement'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #2 or child 2: 'Keys are stored in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (format: `environments: {name: <string>, public_key: <OpenSSH ed25519 pubkey>}`), protected by the same Gerrit/branch-protection rule as CI config; the test fixture creates this file directly.'

findings.finding: The plan reuses the identity epic's commit-ancestry era mechanism for environment-key rotation but never defines the environment keyring record schema (field names analogous to added_at_commit / revoked_at_commit / public_key) or its storage location — only stating it is 'out-of-band' and 'never the tickets branch'.
findings.location: Approach section ('Environment-key rotation reuses the era-scoped key-validity mechanism… applied to the environment keyring'); AC4 ('Environment-key rotation uses era-scoped validity… reusing the commit-ancestry era mechanism')
findings.suggested_fix: Reference OpenSSH allowed_signers valid-after/valid-before as the environment keyring format (which sshsig.py already depends on for version >= 8.9), stored at a named Gerrit-protected path, with a defined canonical section for each environment's entry.

findings.finding: The `rebar.opcert.v1` certificate payload schema — the fields, envelope shape, and value vocabulary an executor must produce and verify — is referenced by namespace string throughout the plan but never defined. No field names, required properties, or type constraints appear in the plan text, and no such schema exists in the codebase.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria #1 / Approach 'Environment-attributed, asymmetric op-certs'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Certificate payload schema' subsection to the Approach section enumerating the required fields and their types, the DSSE payloadType URI (e.g., `application/vnd.rebar.opcert.v1+json`), and which fields are covered by the Ed25519 signature.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
